text.title: Spec Requirements Checklist
text.summary: Phase-by-phase checklist for authoring a complete spec.md from a feature brief.

rule.statement: Before writing any requirement, create a Business Value Statement: [WHO] currently cannot [WHAT] because [WHY]. This results in [IMPACT]. By implementing [SOLUTION], we will [BENEFIT].
rule.rationale: Agents and reviewers need the "why" anchor before requirements; without it, Achievable and Relevant criteria cannot be validated.

list.item: Specific: identify exactly which persona has which problem
Measurable: define the success metric (not "feels good" — a number)
Achievable: confirm technical feasibility before writing the requirement
Relevant: link to an actual business goal, not a nice-to-have
Time-bound: assign a MoSCoW priority before moving to user stories
Create minimum 2 distinct personas (name, role, goal, pain points, context)
Personas must differ — not duplicates with different names
Include when/where they use the product (device, environment)
Personas must be specific enough to make design decisions from
Identify high-level user activities (e.g., "Manage Account")
Create backbone: main actions under each activity
Map walking skeleton: MVP stories under each backbone item
Map phase-2 stories separately from MVP — show swimlane boundaries
Verify MVP is independently launchable without phase-2 stories
Format: "As a [persona], I want [action], so that [benefit]" — never omit the benefit clause
Independent: can be delivered without requiring another story in the same sprint
Negotiable: implementation details can be discussed, goal is fixed
Valuable: delivers measurable user or business value on its own
Estimable: team can size it — if not, split further
Small: fits one iteration; if not, split
Testable: at least one AC can be written for it

rule.statement: Write use cases only for multi-step workflows with multiple actors or complex branching. Single-actor, linear flows do not need a use case — a user story with AC is sufficient.
rule.rationale: Use case overhead on simple features adds documentation burden without improving clarity.

list.item: Primary actor, preconditions, main flow (numbered), alternative flows (3a, 4a format), postconditions
Alternative flows must cover validation errors and edge cases
One requirement per FR-X — no compound requirements ("X and Y")
"System SHALL" imperative; no ambiguous words
Include validation rules if input constraints exist
Trace to US-X in the FR body
Assign MoSCoW priority; Must must not exceed 60% of FR count
Performance: response time with percentile and load conditions
Scalability: concurrent user or throughput ceiling
Security: auth mechanism, encryption standard, compliance (GDPR, etc.)
Availability: uptime SLA with RTO/RPO
Accessibility: WCAG level if user-facing
Each NFR: specific target + measurement method + priority
Given-When-Then with specific values (email "test@example.com", not "valid email")
AC-001: happy path
AC-002: validation error
AC-003: boundary condition
AC-004: security scenario (unauthorized access)
AC-005: performance scenario (if Must NFR applies)
In Scope: explicit list of what IS included
Out of Scope: explicit list with reason and timeline for each item
"Not in this release" without a reason is not sufficient — add "Phase 2", "Never", or "v2.0"
Document assumptions about user behavior, system state, external services
Technical constraints: file size limits, API rate limits, browser support
Business constraints: budget, timeline, integration dependencies
Validate assumptions with stakeholders before signing off spec
